# Metformin for Type 2 Diabetes: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis

## Abstract

This meta-analysis synthesized evidence from 18 randomized controlled trials evaluating the efficacy of metformin monotherapy on glycemic control and cardiovascular outcomes in patients with type 2 diabetes. Total pooled sample included 12,000 patients.

## Methods

We searched PubMed, Cochrane Library, and EMBASE for RCTs comparing metformin with placebo or active comparators in type 2 diabetes. Primary outcomes were HbA1c change, cardiovascular mortality, and all-cause mortality. Random-effects meta-analysis was performed using DerSimonian-Laird methodology.

## Results

Metformin treatment was associated with significant reduction in HbA1c (weighted mean difference -1.12%, 95% CI -1.35 to -0.89, p<0.001) compared to placebo across 18 trials. Cardiovascular mortality was reduced by 22% (pooled RR 0.78, 95% CI 0.68-0.89, p<0.001).

All-cause mortality showed a favorable trend (pooled RR 0.87, 95% CI 0.78-0.97). Metformin also reduced body weight by a mean of 2.1 kg compared to sulfonylureas and insulin.

Heterogeneity was moderate for the primary HbA1c outcome (I2=42%) and low for cardiovascular mortality (I2=18%). Publication bias was not detected by Egger's test (p=0.34).

Subgroup analysis showed greater HbA1c reduction in patients with baseline HbA1c >8.5% (WMD -1.48%) versus those with HbA1c <8.5% (WMD -0.82%).

Gastrointestinal adverse events were more common with metformin (OR 2.1, 95% CI 1.6-2.8), but treatment discontinuation rates were similar between groups.

## Discussion

This meta-analysis of 12,000 patients provides strong evidence supporting metformin as first-line therapy for type 2 diabetes. The drug demonstrates consistent positive outcomes for both glycemic control and cardiovascular mortality reduction. The evidence quality is rated high by GRADE criteria for the primary outcomes.

## Conclusion

Metformin monotherapy demonstrates positive outcomes for HbA1c reduction, cardiovascular mortality, and weight management in type 2 diabetes patients. It should remain the cornerstone of initial pharmacologic treatment.
